one_line_takeaway: TRACE replaces pixel-flipping or codebook glyph-substitution in document watermarking with diffusion-guided structural edits to character skeletons, beating the SOTA font-based baseline AutoStegaFont on extraction accuracy (often by 10-25 points) and PSNR/SSIM across five fonts and three cross-media attack channels (screenshot, print-scan, print-camera), while remaining robust to an elastic structure-morphing adaptive attack.
key_finding: TRACE outperforms ASF across every tested font/size/distortion combination in Tables 1-3. Representative numbers (ASF -> TRACE): Arial 12pt screenshots 85.83% -> 96.67%; TNR 12pt print-scan 64.17% -> 92.50%; Calibri 12pt print-camera 71.67% -> 96.67%; several cells reach 100% ACC for TRACE at larger sizes where ASF plateaus around 80-98%. Against print-camera angle/distance variation (Fig. 4), TRACE stays >80% accuracy up to a 60-degree shooting angle vs. ASF dropping to ~50%, and stays >90% across 20-60cm distances vs. ASF ~60%. Imperceptibility (Table 5): TRACE achieves PSNR 32.4-41.0 dB and SSIM 0.995-0.998 across the five fonts vs. ASF's 26.2-30.3 dB / 0.984-0.989 -- roughly the '>5 dB PSNR' improvement claimed in the abstract. Against image-based baselines on Calibri (Table 4), TRACE reaches 98.75-100% ACC across screenshot/print-scan/print-camera with PSNR 33.34 / SSIM 0.9962, beating StegaStamp (PSNR 27.19/SSIM 0.8986) and IHA (84.6-84.0% ACC, PSNR 29.60/SSIM 0.9910). Generalization results (Table 6) show TRACE also works on handwritten fonts (91.7-94.4% ACC, PSNR 38.20) and artistic fonts (92.9-97.3% ACC, PSNR 41.37), and qualitatively on Korean/Japanese/Russian/Arabic scripts and mathematical formulas (Appendix G, no quantitative ACC given there). Ablation (Table 7): full MPE+TPE gives 100% ACC; MPE alone 68.75%; TPE alone 49.95%; neither ~53.5% (near-random). MPE rule ablation (Table A) shows removing any of the three scoring rules drops ACC to 63-81% and/or breaks PSNR/SSIM thresholds. Against the structure-morphing adaptive attack (Table 8), TRACE retains 96.5-99.96% ACC across 12-36pt at both Light and Strong deformation strength.
core_contribution: TRACE is a document/character-level watermarking framework that embeds bits by editing the glyph *structure* (skeleton keypoints) of characters using a diffusion model, rather than flipping pixels (image-based methods) or substituting glyphs from a pre-defined codebook (font-based methods). The embedding pipeline has three original components: (1) Adaptive Diffusion Initialization (ADI), which auto-selects a 'handle point' via a Movement Probability Evaluator (MPE, a scoring rule over stroke endpoints/junctions), computes a 'target point' via Target Point Estimation (TPE, based on stroke direction), and draws an editing mask via a Mask Drawing Module (MDM); (2) Guided Diffusion Encoding (GDE), which fine-tunes a diffusion UNet with LoRA and uses DragDiffusion-style motion supervision plus a new local-consistency loss (L_lc) to move the handle point to the target point while preserving local shape; and (3) Masked Region Replacement (MRR), which pastes the edited mask region back onto the original image to limit feature drift outside the edit. Extraction re-runs MPE/keypoint detection on segmented characters and thresholds the handle-reference point distance to recover the bit.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section 3.1. Two parties: a document owner who watermarks documents for provenance tracing/copyright protection, and an adversary who redistributes watermarked documents without authorization through cross-media channels (print-scan, print-camera capture, screenshots). The adversary is assumed to know the high-level design of TRACE (structure-based embedding) but not the specific embedding parameters (e.g., thresholds, D, tau), and may mount adaptive attacks -- specifically the paper evaluates a 'structure morphing attack' (elastic deformation of character strokes) at Light/Strong strength, plus generic image processing (Gaussian noise/blur) and a 'letter comparing' attack where the adversary directly diffs encoded vs. original glyphs, which the paper counters by encrypting the message with a 128-bit key before embedding (not a novel embedding-robustness result, just standard encryption of payload).
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges: (1) a fundamental 'letter comparing' vulnerability -- because text images have sparse pixel distributions, an adversary who directly diffs the encoded vs. original glyph can potentially detect or infer the embedded bit, especially for codebook-based competitors; TRACE's own mitigation is generic payload encryption (128-bit key) rather than a structural fix. (2) Efficiency is explicitly stated as 'not the main focus' -- LoRA fine-tuning (~30s) plus latent optimization (~1 min) per character is expensive versus codebook lookup methods, requiring either precomputing a codebook (limiting the claimed per-character generality) or accepting slower per-document embedding; swapping in InstantDrag trades some invisibility (-0.7dB PSNR) for speed. (3) The threat model assumes the adversary does not know exact embedding parameters (thresholds, D, tau) -- an adversary with full white-box knowledge of these parameters is not evaluated. (4) Generalization results to non-Latin/non-Chinese scripts and math formulas are shown only qualitatively (Fig. C/D) without extraction-accuracy tables, so the claimed 'broad generalizability' is not quantitatively verified for those scripts to the same rigor as the main English/Chinese results.

datasets_used: Self-constructed English dataset: Arial, Times New Roman, Calibri fonts, 6 sizes (12/16/20/24/28/36pt), 60 text images per font-size cell (uppercase+lowercase letters), 120 embedding samples (0-bit and 1-bit) per cell
Self-constructed Chinese dataset: SimSun and SimHei fonts, 60 most-frequent Chinese characters (selected via ChatGPT), same 6 sizes, 120 samples per cell
Handwritten and artistic font samples (Table 6, qualitative set for generalizability)
Korean, Japanese, Russian, Arabic character samples and 3 mathematical formulas (Appendix G, qualitative only)
quantitative_results: Robustness vs ASF across Tables 1-3 (screenshots/print-scan/print-camera, 5 fonts x 6 sizes): TRACE consistently higher ACC, e.g. Arial 12pt print-scan 80.83%(ASF) vs 95.83%(TRACE); TNR 12pt print-camera 65.83% vs 90.00%

vs image-based baselines on Calibri (Table 4): TRACE screenshot/print-scan/print-camera ACC 100/99.05/98.75%, PSNR 33.34, SSIM 0.9962; StegaStamp 100/98.54/98.12%, PSNR 27.19, SSIM 0.8986; IHA 84.58/84.29/83.94%, PSNR 29.60, SSIM 0.9910
Print-camera angle robustness (Fig.4): TRACE >80% ACC up to 60-degree angle vs ASF ~50%; distance 20-60cm TRACE >90% vs ASF ~60%
Handwritten/artistic fonts (Table 6): Handwritten 94.43/93.17/91.67% (screenshot/print-scan/print-camera), PSNR 38.20, SSIM 0.996; Artistic 97.27/94.77/92.93%, PSNR 41.37, SSIM 0.995
MPE/TPE ablation (Table 7): both ON = 100% ACC; MPE only 68.75%; TPE only 49.95%; neither 53.50%
MPE rule ablation (Table A): full MPE PSNR 32.10/SSIM 0.962/ACC 100.00%; w/o Rule1 25.24/0.911/63.33%; w/o Rule2 39.48/0.997/80.67%; w/o Rule3 32.82/0.965/68.51%
Structure morphing adaptive attack (Table 8): ACC 96.54-99.96% across 12-36pt, Light and Strong strength
Common image-processing attack (Gaussian noise var 0.03, 3x3 blur, 12pt Calibri): 100% extraction accuracy retained

Efficiency (Appendix F, RTX 4090, 512x512): LoRA fine-tuning ~30s, latent optimization ~1 min, ADI negligible (0.01-0.015s), extraction ~0.005s; InstantDrag variant embedding time 0.704s with PSNR -0.7dB, SSIM -0.0002 vs DragDiffusion version
baselines_compared: AutoStegaFont (ASF) -- SOTA font-based codebook watermarking, primary baseline across Tables 1-3, 5, 7
